original_abstract: The proliferation of artificial intelligence across all sectors of society presents significant opportunities alongside profound risks to fundamental rights, health, and safety. In response, the European Union has enacted Regulation (EU) 2024/1689, commonly known as the AI Act—the world’s first comprehensive legal framework for AI governance. This paper examines the architecture of the Act, which shifts away from non-binding ethics guidelines toward a mandatory, risk-based regulatory approach. Central to the Regulation is a classification system that categorizes AI applications into levels of risk: prohibiting practices deemed an "unacceptable risk" to EU values, imposing strict requirements on "high-risk" systems, and establishing specific transparency obligations for generative AI and chatbots. Furthermore, the paper analyzes the Act’s late-stage incorporation of rules for general-purpose AI models and systemic risks, spurred by the rapid ascent of large-scale generative models. By addressing enforcement mechanisms, international extraterritorial effects, and the goal of harmonizing the European internal market, this study considers how the AI Act aims to foster human-centric innovation while establishing a global benchmark for trustworthy artificial intelligence.
humanized_abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) is everywhere and its impact on fundamental rights, human health and safety is unprecedented. The European Union (EU) has now adopted Regulation (EU) 2024/1689, the so-called AI Act, which has been called a historic and pioneering regulation — the first law in the world to govern the use of AI. This column discusses the general outline of the Act as it moves from ethics guidelines to a mandatory, risk-based framework. It provides an analysis of the composition of the Act and specifically the risk classification, which divides all uses of AI into three categories: prohibits practices that pose an unacceptable risk to the values recognised in Article 2 of the Treaty on European United (TEU); provides strict guidelines for ‘high risk’ uses of AI, which have to assess the impact on human rights, pay particular attention to the risk of bias, gender, discrimination and inequalities, and guarantee transparency and explainability; provides specific disclosure requirements for generative AI and chatbots and discusses the relatively late adoption of some of the provisions on large language models (LLMs) and other systemic risks that have evolved dramatically over the last year, spurred by the dramatic evolution of the latter over the last year. It discusses how the Act addresses the governance of the enforcement mechanisms and the extra-territorial implications of the law and how it can be considered a global standard reference for fair and responsible AI development. It also analyses the potential for the Act to achieve the ambitious goals of ensuring that a human-centred innovation continues and that the Act serves as an opportunity to consolidate the single market in the EU.
